title: ShadowLab: Building a Python C2 Prototype for Security Labs (V1.3)
content:
I built **ShadowLab**: A modular, Python-based C2 framework designed for security research and offensive simulation. 

Traditional cybersecurity study often stops at analyzing existing tools; I believe true expertise comes from building the architecture from scratch to understand the underlying mechanics of EDR evasion and network protocols. This is a technical retrospective on the engineering choices behind ShadowLab.

![Demonstration of the ShadowLab C2 server interacting with the Python agent](https://dev-to-uploads.s3.amazonaws.com/uploads/articles/pm792vdnsohddft7jqvl.gif)

### üèóÔ∏è Architectural Foundations: The "Clean" Approach
ShadowLab follows **Clean Architecture** principles, decoupling the server's core logic to ensure maintainability and scalability. Instead of a monolithic script, the framework is structured into specialized modules:

* **Controller (C2 Server):** The central hub responsible for command dispatching, cryptographic key management, and session monitoring.
* **Agent (Implant):** A lightweight binary designed for minimal footprint, supporting both *Staged* (bootstrap downloader) and *Unstaged* (monolithic) execution modes.
### üèõÔ∏è Project Structure
```plaintext
ShadowLab/
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ Shadow.py                 # Main C2 Server Application
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ requirements.txt          # Python Package Dependencies
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ LICENSE                   # Project License File
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ SECURITY.md               # Security Policy
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ FAQS.md                   # Frequently Asked Questions
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ CONTRIBUTING.md           # Contribution Guidelines
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ README.md                 # Project Documentation
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ assets/                   # Media & Resources
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ confs/                    # Configuration Files
‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ conf.json             # Encryption Keys & Server Settings
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ mainclass/                # Core Server Modules
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ builder.py            # Agent/Payload Builder
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ comm.py               # Network Communication Handler
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ encrypter.py          # Encryption & Decryption Utilities
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ pyi_progress.py       # PyInstaller Integration & Progress Display
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ options.py            # Command-Line Options & Menus
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ shell.py              # Remote Command Handlers
‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ system.py             # System Utilities & Display
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ payloads/                 # Agent/Implant Code
‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ payload.py            # Unstaged Payload (Full-Featured)
‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ payload_staged.py     # Staged Payload (Lightweight)
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ postexploits/             # Post-Exploitation Modules (Future)
‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ keystroke.py          # (In Development ‚Äî Pending Security Review)
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ photos/                   # Screenshot & Image Storage Directory
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ records/                  # Audio Recording Storage Directory
‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ build/                    # PyInstaller Build Output Directory
```

{% embed https://gist.github.com/msalihberk/a27403beb9581d32a94cf585700b20e8 %}


### üõ°Ô∏è Hardened Communication & Network Layer
The network layer is the most critical component. To ensure confidentiality and stability, I implemented:

* **End-to-End Encryption:** All packets are secured using `Fernet` (AES-128) with dynamic key management.
* **Length-Prefixed Protocols:** To mitigate TCP streaming anomalies like packet fragmentation, I integrated a 4-byte length-prefixed protocol. 
* **Non-Blocking I/O:** Using Python's native networking structures, the communication handler manages concurrent socket operations effectively.



### üìä Deployment Modes Comparison

| Mode | Use Case | Detection Risk |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Staged** | Bootstrap/Downloader | Low (Smaller footprint) |
| **Unstaged** | Self-contained binary | Moderate (Monolithic) |

Check out the repository, and I would highly appreciate any backend architecture reviews or code quality feedback from the infosec community here!
